title: Space Tech Briefing — May 13, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space cadets and Earth-bound explorers! Welcome to your daily dose of cosmic currents, live on this magnificent May 13, 2026. I'm your host, ready to launch you into the latest headlines from across the universe and beyond!

First up, an inspiring story from the French space agency, CNES. Their inaugural national "Astronaut for a Day" educational project has just reached its exciting finale! This incredible initiative captivated over 2,000 middle school students, challenging them with astronaut-style selections. Now, after rigorous stages, 100 aspiring spacefarers have made it to the final round. What an extraordinary adventure for these young minds, truly propelling them towards a future in space!

Next, we're tracking a critical mission to save a vital eye on the cosmos. NASA's Neil Gehrels Swift space telescope, launched way back in 2004, has been diligently observing the Universe from its low-Earth orbit, initially at 600 kilometers altitude. Swift is a multi-wavelength powerhouse, specifically designed to detect and study gamma-ray bursts – those incredibly violent, high-energy flashes that illuminate the most extreme events in the Universe. It's given us thousands of precise light curves and confirmed the existence of two main families of these bursts. But Swift is gradually losing altitude. Without intervention, it faces re-entry and would be lost. The good news? An unprecedented mission called "Swift Boost" is slated for 2026 to re-elevate its orbit and extend its invaluable operational life. Here's to many more years of groundbreaking discoveries from Swift!

And finally, for those of you on Earth, particularly in Paris, the CNES is also a proud partner in a fascinating temporary exhibition. It's titled "Explorations: A State Affair?" and it's currently on display at the Army Museum Invalides. This exhibition takes visitors on a journey through three centuries of French scientific and military adventures, retracing incredible tales of exploration across both time and space. A wonderful opportunity to connect our past with our cosmic future!

That's all for your cosmic briefing this May 13, 2026. Thank you for tuning in! Stay curious, keep looking up, and we'll catch you back here tomorrow for more space news. Have a stellar day!
